skill.name: image-inpainting
skill.description: Use RunComfy image inpainting models for precise, mask-driven local edits. The skill guides the runcomfy CLI for object cleanup, region replacement, background repair, and description-based fallbacks when no mask exists.
skill.summary: Run mask-driven image inpainting and local image edits through the RunComfy CLI.
skill.author: agentspace-so

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high-risk patterns, but the strongest alerts are mostly false positives from Markdown backticks, model names, and documentation text. Real confirmed risks remain: the skill intentionally invokes the RunComfy CLI, sends prompts and image URLs to RunComfy network endpoints, and documents persistent local token storage. No evidence of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, hidden installer execution, or prompt injection against the evaluator was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: External RunComfy CLI Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate purpose. The skill explicitly allows Bash(runcomfy *) and provides runcomfy run examples. This is expected for the skill, but it still executes an external CLI with user-supplied prompts and image URLs, so users should review commands before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution is directly documented and scoped to runcomfy. I did not find evidence that arbitrary shell commands or prompt text are shell-expanded.

medium_findings.title: Outbound Image and Prompt Processing
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate purpose. The skill sends image URLs, mask URLs, and prompt content to RunComfy model APIs. This is required for remote generation, but it creates privacy risk for sensitive images or proprietary prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The networking behavior is explicitly described: the CLI posts to the Model API and polls for results. The documented endpoint allowlist reduces concern, but data still leaves the local environment.

medium_findings.title: Persistent Local API Token Storage
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with legitimate purpose. The skill states that runcomfy login writes an API token to a hidden user configuration path. The documented 0600 mode is a mitigation, but local credential persistence remains a security consideration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token path and permissions are explicitly documented on one line. This appears normal for a CLI login flow, not malicious credential harvesting.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The scanner classified many Markdown inline-code spans and fenced bash examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. SKILL.md is documentation and contains no Ruby source or executable backtick substitution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is visible in Markdown prose and bash code fences. No evidence found of Ruby code or runtime backtick execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The flagged lines discuss image masks, model routing, exit codes, and CLI behavior. No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithms, hashing code, host reconnaissance commands, or collection of system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The source file is documentation for image editing workflows, not implementation code. The static labels do not match the visible content at the cited lines.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious networking. The hardcoded URLs point to RunComfy documentation, RunComfy model pages, skills.sh references, and placeholder user CDN examples. They support documentation and model routing rather than hidden exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are human-readable documentation and allowlist entries. The only operational network risk is already captured in the medium finding for RunComfy API processing.

dangerous_patterns.title: Scoped Bash Tool Permission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill grants Bash(runcomfy *) permission. This is narrower than unrestricted shell access, but it still permits invoking an external command-line tool.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool scope is explicit in the frontmatter and reiterated in the security section. The scope appears intentionally constrained to runcomfy.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Model API Submission
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill describes CLI submission to RunComfy model APIs with image, mask, and prompt inputs. Sensitive media should not be processed without user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is directly stated in the documentation. The line also claims an endpoint allowlist and no telemetry, which lowers but does not remove privacy risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Token File
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents a token file under the user configuration directory. File mode 0600 is appropriate, but persistent credentials can be exposed by local compromise or careless workspace sharing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact token storage path is documented. There is no evidence in SKILL.md that the token is transmitted outside normal RunComfy authentication.

content.user_title: Remove Objects with Masked Image Inpainting
content.value_statement: Manual retouching is slow when a photo needs precise local edits. This skill routes masked inpainting jobs through RunComfy models with clear CLI inputs.
actual_capabilities: Routes mask-based edits to Tongyi MAI Z-Image Turbo Inpainting.
Provides runcomfy CLI examples for object removal and region replacement.
Explains strength and control scale choices for cleanup or full replacement.
Supports description-based fallback models when no mask is available.
Documents model choices for LoRA style edits, GPT Image 2 Edit, and FLUX Kontext Pro.
Lists RunComfy CLI exit codes and troubleshooting references.
limitations: Requires the RunComfy CLI and a valid RunComfy login or token.
Mask-driven Z-Image inpainting requires a source image URL and mask image URL.
The skill does not create masks; users need an upstream mask creation tool.
Remote model processing may be unsuitable for confidential or regulated images.

use_cases.title: Repair Product Photos
use_cases.description: Remove distractions or replace a masked background while preserving the product and lighting.

use_cases.title: Clean Marketing Images
use_cases.description: Patch small visual defects and keep surrounding textures consistent with the original image.

use_cases.title: Prototype Local Edits
use_cases.description: Compare mask-based and description-based edit routes before building a repeatable image pipeline.

prompt_templates.title: Remove a Small Object
prompt_templates.prompt: Inpaint this image using my mask. Remove the selected object and preserve the surrounding texture, lighting, and perspective.

prompt_templates.title: Replace a Background Region
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the masked background region only. Replace it with a smooth studio backdrop and keep the subject unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Match a Brand Style
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the inpainting LoRA route for the masked region. Match the provided brand style while preserving all unmasked pixels.

prompt_templates.title: Choose the Best Edit Route
prompt_templates.prompt: I have this image and edit goal. Decide whether to use mask-based inpainting or description-based editing, then prepare the RunComfy command.

output_examples.input: A street photo with a mask over overhead cables.
output_examples.output: A RunComfy inpainting plan that removes the cables, preserves rooflines, and uses moderate strength for natural sky repair.

output_examples.input: A product image with the background masked.
output_examples.output: A region replacement workflow that swaps the background for light gray studio paper while keeping the product unchanged.

output_examples.input: A photo with no mask but a clear unwanted object in one corner.
output_examples.output: A recommendation to use a description-based edit model with spatial instructions and preservation constraints.

best_practices: Use masks that match the source image dimensions and soften hard edges slightly.
State what must stay unchanged outside the masked region.
Use only images and masks that the user explicitly provided or approved.
anti_patterns: Do not process confidential images through remote APIs without explicit approval.
Do not use description-based editing when pixel-level mask accuracy is required.
Do not remove ownership marks or protected content unless the user has rights to edit them.

faq.question: Does this skill create the mask for me?
faq.answer: No. It consumes a mask URL. Create the mask with an image editor or segmentation tool first.

faq.question: Which model is the default route?
faq.answer: The default route is Tongyi MAI Z-Image Turbo Inpainting when a mask is available.

faq.question: Can I use it without a mask?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill recommends description-based edit models when the target area can be described clearly.

faq.question: Does it run locally?
faq.answer: No. The runcomfy CLI sends work to RunComfy model APIs and downloads the result.

faq.question: What inputs should I prepare?
faq.answer: Prepare a source image URL, a mask image URL for mask-based edits, and a clear prompt.

faq.question: Is it safe for private photos?
faq.answer: Use caution. Remote processing may not be appropriate for sensitive, private, or regulated images.
